subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look down at the ground beneath your feet. Right now you are standing on the outside of an enormous ball of rock and metal that is spinning through space. We call it the Earth. It is our home, and it is the only planet we know of where people, animals, plants, camels, birds and fish can all live together. In this unit you will become an Earth explorer. You will discover what the Earth is like deep inside, why some mountains spit out fire and hot rock, why the ground sometimes shakes, and how so many different living things find a home in different corners of our world.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Describe the three main layers of the Earth - the crust, the mantle and the core - and say what each layer is made of.
Explain how scientists use models to understand the inside of the Earth, and why a model is never a perfect copy of the real thing.
Describe how a volcano forms and name its main parts: the vent, the crater, the lava and the ash.
Explain what causes an earthquake and how the shaking travels as waves to the surface.
Describe how earthquakes under the sea can cause a tsunami (a giant sea wave).
Explain how animals and plants are suited to their habitat, using local examples such as the savanna, the coral reef and the mangrove.
Connect the Earth's hot inside layers to the volcanoes and earthquakes we see at the surface.
Here are the important words for this unit. Read them now, and come back to this table whenever you meet a word you have forgotten.
The thin, solid outer layer of the Earth where we live, made of rock.

concepts.title: The Structure of the Earth
concepts.explanation: You already know quite a lot about the outside of the Earth. It is made of rocks, soil, sand, mountains, and the salty water of the seas and oceans. Scientists call this the Earth's external structure - 'external' just means 'outside'. When you walk across the savanna near Garissa, or feel the sand of the Indian Ocean coast at Kismaayo, you are touching the external structure of the Earth. But what about the parts we cannot see - the parts deep inside the Earth? These make up the Earth's internal structure…
concepts.example: The answer is that scientists use a model. A model is a way of showing or explaining something that we cannot easily see. You use models all the time without knowing it. A toy camel is a model of a real camel. A drawing…

concepts.title: Volcanoes
concepts.explanation: Now that you know there is hot, melted magma inside the mantle, you can understand volcanoes. Sometimes a big crack develops in the Earth's crust. Because the magma below is under great pressure, it pushes its way up through that crack. When the magma finally bursts out onto the surface, we say the volcano erupts. Once the magma has reached the surface and is flowing in the open air, we give it a new name: lava. It is the same red-hot rock, just with a different name depending on whether it is inside the Earth…
concepts.example: Why does the magma push upwards at all? Think about what happens when you squeeze the bottom of a tube of toothpaste: the paste has nowhere to go but up and out of the opening. In a similar way, the weight of all the…

concepts.title: Earthquakes
concepts.explanation: The word 'quake' means to shake. So an earthquake is a sudden shaking of the ground. Rub your two hands together quickly right now. Can you feel them getting warm? That warmth is energy made by the two surfaces rubbing. Something similar, but on a giant scale, causes an earthquake. Two special words help us describe an earthquake. The focus is the exact spot inside the crust where the earthquake begins - where the rocks first slip. The epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus. The shaking is…
concepts.example: Here is another way to picture how the energy stores up and then lets go. Take a fresh green twig from a tree and slowly bend it. At first it just bends and holds, storing up the pushing energy in your hands. But if you…

concepts.title: Different Habitats
concepts.explanation: The Earth is not only rock and fire. Its surface is covered with living things, and every plant and animal lives in the place that suits it best. A habitat is the natural home of a living thing - the place where it finds the food, the water, the shelter and the space it needs to survive. Living things have special features, called adaptations, that make them suited to their own habitat. Think about a fish, such as the tilapia that swims in the Jubba River or in Rift Valley lakes. A fish cannot live out of water,…
concepts.example: Birds have wings for flying, but they are also suited to their habitats in another clever way: the shape of their beak matches the food they eat. The habitat a bird lives in decides what food is available, and the beak…

explorations.title: Model the Layers of the Earth
explorations.context: To build a model that shows the three layers of the Earth - crust, mantle and core - using food from your kitchen.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: which food could stand for the hard core in the middle? Why do you think so?
explorations.answer: Which layer was the thinnest in your model - the crust, the mantle or the core? Is the real Earth's core soft like a fruit stone, or is it hot metal? What does this tell you about how a model is different from the real thing? Name one way your fruit is a GOOD model of the Earth, and one way it is a…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a model that shows the three layers of the Earth - crust, mantle and core - using food from your kitchen.

explorations.title: Build an Erupting Volcano
explorations.context: To model how a volcano erupts, using a safe reaction between baking soda and vinegar.
explorations.prompt: Write down: what do you think will happen when you pour the vinegar onto the baking soda? Will it be fast or slow?
explorations.answer: The bubbling foam is like the lava. In a real volcano, where does the lava come from deep inside the Earth? The bottle opening is like the vent. What is the vent's job in a real volcano? Was your eruption fast or slow? Does this match what you predicted in your hypothesis?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To model how a volcano erupts, using a safe reaction between baking soda and vinegar.

explorations.title: The Jelly Earthquake Shake Test
explorations.context: To see how the shaking waves of an earthquake can make buildings fall, and to find out what kind of building stands up best.
explorations.prompt: Write down: do you think a tall, thin tower or a short, wide tower will fall over first when the ground shakes?
explorations.answer: Which tower fell more easily - the tall thin one or the short wide one? The wobbling jelly is like the crust during an earthquake. Why is it useful to repeat the test several times instead of just once? How could this help people design safer buildings in places that have earthquakes, such as towns…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To see how the shaking waves of an earthquake can make buildings fall, and to find out what kind of building stands up best.

explorations.title: Bird Beak and Habitat Watch
explorations.context: To observe real birds near your home and discover how their beaks are suited to the food in their habitat.
explorations.prompt: Write down: do you think birds with different beak shapes eat different foods? What makes you think so?
explorations.answer: Did birds with different beak shapes seem to eat different foods? Give an example. Why did watching on more than one day give you more reliable data? Choose one bird and explain how its beak is suited to its habitat.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe real birds near your home and discover how their beaks are suited to the food in their habitat.

methods.title: Model the Layers of the Earth
methods.example: To build a model that shows the three layers of the Earth - crust, mantle and core - using food from your kitchen.
steps: Look at the whole fruit. This smooth outside is like the Earth's surface.
Ask an adult to carefully cut the fruit in half so you can see inside. (Safety note: only an adult should use the knife.)
Find the three parts. The thin skin is the crust. The thick soft fruit is the mantle. The hard stone in the middle is the core.
Point to each part and say its name out loud.
Draw your cut fruit and label the crust, mantle and core.

methods.title: Build an Erupting Volcano
methods.example: To model how a volcano erupts, using a safe reaction between baking soda and vinegar.
steps: Stand the bottle in the middle of the tray. If you like, pack sand, soil or dough around it to make it look like a mountain, leaving the bottle opening clear at the top.
Put 2 spoons of baking soda into the bottle.
Add a squeeze of dish soap and a few drops of red food colouring, if you have them.
Now the eruption! Pour the vinegar into the bottle and step back to watch. (Safety note: pour gently and keep your face away - the foam can splash. Do not put your eyes close to the opening.)
Watch the red foam bubble up and flow down the sides like lava.

methods.title: The Jelly Earthquake Shake Test
methods.example: To see how the shaking waves of an earthquake can make buildings fall, and to find out what kind of building stands up best.
steps: Place the plate of jelly (or the cushion) on a table. This is your patch of ground.
On top of the jelly, carefully build a tall, thin tower with your blocks.
Next to it, build a short, wide tower using the same number of blocks.
Gently shake or tap the plate from the side to send 'earthquake waves' through the jelly.
Slowly shake a little harder. Watch which tower wobbles and falls first.

methods.title: Bird Beak and Habitat Watch
methods.example: To observe real birds near your home and discover how their beaks are suited to the food in their habitat.
steps: Sit or stand very still and quietly in your chosen spot. (Safety note: stay in a place your family knows is safe, and do not wander near deep water or busy roads.)
Watch carefully and try to spot at least three different birds.
For each bird, look at the shape of its beak - is it small and pointed, long and sharp, flat, or curved?
Notice what the bird is eating or looking for: seeds, insects, fish, or nectar.
Draw the shape of each beak in your notebook.

workedExamples.title: Comparing the Layers
workedExamples.prompt: Sometimes in science we compare numbers to understand size. Let us work through an example together, step by step.
workedExamples.solution: Question: The mantle is about 3000 km deep. The ocean crust is about 5 km thick. About how many times thicker is the mantle than the ocean crust? Step 1 - Write down what we know. Mantle depth = 3000 km. Ocean crust thickness = 5 km. Step 2 - Decide what to do. To find out 'how many times thicker', we divide the bigger number by the smaller number. Step 3 - Do the division. 3000 divided by 5 = 600.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: The very hot centre of the Earth is made mostly of: (a) rock (b) water (c) iron and nickel (d) ice
workedExamples.solution: 2 (c) iron and nickel The core is made of these two metals, not rock.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Melted rock inside the Earth is called: (a) lava (b) ash (c) magma (d) crust
workedExamples.solution: 3 (c) magma Melted rock inside the Earth is magma.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Once melted rock erupts onto the surface, we call it: (a) magma (b) lava (c) core (d) mantle
workedExamples.solution: 4 (b) lava The same melted rock is called lava once it erupts onto the surface.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: The bowl-shaped hole at the top of a volcano is the: (a) vent (b) crater (c) focus (d) epicentre
workedExamples.solution: 5 (b) crater The crater is the bowl-shaped hole at the top of the volcano.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: An earthquake is best described as: (a) a shaking of the ground (b) a hot spring (c) a type of soil (d) a sea wave
workedExamples.solution: 6 (a) a shaking of the ground 'Quake' means shake; an earthquake is the ground shaking.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The point on the surface directly above where an earthquake begins is the: (a) focus (b) mantle (c) epicentre (d) crater
workedExamples.solution: 7 (c) epicentre The epicentre is on the surface directly above the focus.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A huge sea wave caused by an underwater earthquake is a: (a) tsunami (b) landslide (c) crater (d) tremor
workedExamples.solution: 8 (a) tsunami A tsunami is a giant sea wave from an underwater earthquake or eruption.

practice.answer: 1 (b) crust The crust is the thin, solid outer rocky layer we live on.

practice.prompt: The natural home where an animal finds food and shelter is its: (a) habitat (b) beak (c) gill (d) model
practice.answer: 9 (a) habitat A habitat is where an animal finds food, water and shelter.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A flamingo's flat, sieve-like beak is best for: (a) cracking seeds (b) catching fish (c) straining tiny food from water (d) digging for worms
practice.answer: 10 (c) straining tiny food from water The flamingo's flat beak works like a sieve.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The giant crack in the Earth's crust running through East Africa is the: (a) Pacific Ring of Fire (b) Great Rift Valley (c) Indian Ocean (d) Jubba River
practice.answer: 11 (b) Great Rift Valley This giant crack runs through East Africa.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a benefit of volcanoes? (a) they bury crops (b) they make rich farming soil (c) they poison the air (d) they cause landslides
practice.answer: 12 (b) they make rich farming soil Broken-down lava and ash make very fertile soil.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Model the Layers of the Earth
activities.materials: One round fruit with a stone in the middle, such as a mango, peach or plum (the stone will be the core). OR a ball of dough or playdough with a marble hidden inside.; A butter knife (ask an adult to do the cutting).; A plate.
steps: Look at the whole fruit. This smooth outside is like the Earth's surface.
Ask an adult to carefully cut the fruit in half so you can see inside. (Safety note: only an adult should use the knife.)
Find the three parts. The thin skin is the crust. The thick soft fruit is the mantle. The hard stone in the middle is the core.
Point to each part and say its name out loud.
Draw your cut fruit and label the crust, mantle and core.

activities.title: Build an Erupting Volcano
activities.materials: A small empty bottle or cup (this is the vent).; A tray, large plate or baking dish to catch the mess.; Sand, soil or dough to build a mountain shape around the bottle (optional but fun).; 2 spoons of baking soda (bicarbonate of soda).; Half a cup of vinegar.; A squeeze of dish soap and a few drops…
steps: Stand the bottle in the middle of the tray. If you like, pack sand, soil or dough around it to make it look like a mountain, leaving the bottle opening clear at the top.
Put 2 spoons of baking soda into the bottle.
Add a squeeze of dish soap and a few drops of red food colouring, if you have them.
Now the eruption! Pour the vinegar into the bottle and step back to watch. (Safety note: pour gently and keep your face away - the foam can splash. Do not put your eyes close to the opening.)
Watch the red foam bubble up and flow down the sides like lava.

activities.title: The Jelly Earthquake Shake Test
activities.materials: A plate or tray of set jelly (jello), OR a firm cushion or folded towel. This is the wobbly ground (the Earth's crust).; Some small blocks, sugar cubes, dry biscuits, or building bricks to make little towers.; Your hands to make the shaking.
steps: Place the plate of jelly (or the cushion) on a table. This is your patch of ground.
On top of the jelly, carefully build a tall, thin tower with your blocks.
Next to it, build a short, wide tower using the same number of blocks.
Gently shake or tap the plate from the side to send 'earthquake waves' through the jelly.
Slowly shake a little harder. Watch which tower wobbles and falls first.

activities.title: Bird Beak and Habitat Watch
activities.materials: A garden, a field, a rooftop, a riverbank or any open space where birds visit.; A notebook and pencil to record what you see.; Patience and a quiet voice so you do not scare the birds away.
steps: Sit or stand very still and quietly in your chosen spot. (Safety note: stay in a place your family knows is safe, and do not wander near deep water or busy roads.)
Watch carefully and try to spot at least three different birds.
For each bird, look at the shape of its beak - is it small and pointed, long and sharp, flat, or curved?
Notice what the bird is eating or looking for: seeds, insects, fish, or nectar.
Draw the shape of each beak in your notebook.

activities.title: Observe: The Structure of the Earth
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the structure of the earth around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Volcanoes
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of volcanoes around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: that the Earth's crust is not one smooth shell. It is broken into huge pieces of flat rock, like a cracked eggshell. Where two of these giant pieces meet, they push and rub against each other. Usually they are stuck. But pressure builds and builds until suddenly the rocks slip and move. This sudden movement makes a huge amount of energy, and the energy travels outwards as waves. The waves race through the crust to the surface, and when they reach us we feel the ground shake. That is an earthquake.

terms: Crust
The thin, solid rocky outer layer of the Earth where we live.
Mantle
The thick layer below the crust, made of very hot, softened rock.
Magma
Melted rock inside the Earth that can flow slowly.
Lava
Magma that has erupted and now flows on the surface.
Core
The Earth's very hot centre, made of iron and nickel.
Model
A copy or picture that helps us understand something we cannot easily see.
Volcano
A place where magma erupts through the crust onto the surface.
Vent
The central pipe magma travels up inside a volcano.
Crater
The bowl-shaped hole at the top of a volcano.
Ash
Tiny bits of burnt lava thrown into the air during an eruption.
Earthquake
A sudden shaking of the ground caused by rocks slipping in the crust.
Focus
The place inside the crust where an earthquake begins.

vocabulary.meaning: The thin, solid rocky outer layer of the Earth where we live.
vocabulary.example: A giant crack in the Earth's crust, called the Great Rift Valley, runs all the way through Kenya, Ethiopia, Tanzania and beyond.

vocabulary.meaning: The thick layer below the crust, made of very hot, softened rock.
vocabulary.example: Describe the three main layers of the Earth - the crust, the mantle and the core - and say what each layer is made of.

vocabulary.meaning: Melted rock inside the Earth that can flow slowly.
vocabulary.example: Magma sits inside the mantle and rises up inside volcanoes.

vocabulary.meaning: Magma that has erupted and now flows on the surface.
vocabulary.example: Once the magma has reached the surface and is flowing in the open air, we give it a new name: lava.

vocabulary.meaning: The Earth's very hot centre, made of iron and nickel.
vocabulary.example: Describe the three main layers of the Earth - the crust, the mantle and the core - and say what each layer is made of.

vocabulary.meaning: A copy or picture that helps us understand something we cannot easily see.
vocabulary.example: Explain how scientists use models to understand the inside of the Earth, and why a model is never a perfect copy of the real thing.

vocabulary.meaning: A place where magma erupts through the crust onto the surface.
vocabulary.example: Mount Kilimanjaro was built by a volcano long ago.

vocabulary.meaning: The central pipe magma travels up inside a volcano.
vocabulary.example: Vent - the central pipe or tube that the magma travels up through to reach the surface.

vocabulary.meaning: The bowl-shaped hole at the top of a volcano.
vocabulary.example: Crater - the bowl-shaped hole at the very top, where the vent widens and the material bursts out.

vocabulary.meaning: Tiny bits of burnt lava thrown into the air during an eruption.
vocabulary.example: The trapped gases can make the eruption sudden and explosive, throwing ash and rock high into the sky.

vocabulary.meaning: A sudden shaking of the ground caused by rocks slipping in the crust.
vocabulary.example: Explain what causes an earthquake and how the shaking travels as waves to the surface.

vocabulary.meaning: The place inside the crust where an earthquake begins.
vocabulary.example: The focus is the exact spot inside the crust where the earthquake begins - where the rocks first slip.

commonMistakes: Thinking the crust is very thick.
The crust is really thin compared to the Earth.
Thinking magma and lava are different things.
They are the same melted rock.
Thinking earthquakes open up huge cracks that swallow people.
Thinking a tsunami is made by wind.
A tsunami is not a wind wave.
Thinking any animal can live anywhere.
Each animal is suited to a habitat.

connections.text: the rocks of the crust and the rich volcanic soil link to what you learn about materials and how living things grow.
the heat inside the Earth, and the energy that travels as earthquake waves, connect to your work on heat and energy.
habitats and adaptations lead straight into your study of animals, plants and how they survive.
the Great Rift Valley, the Indian Ocean coast and mountains like Kilimanjaro are places you will meet again in geography.
caring for the Earth as a khalifah connects science to your duty to protect Allah's creation.
Find out about a hot spring or a volcano in East Africa and how people use it. You could make a poster.

realProblems.prompt: Farah's family grows bananas on the fertile slopes near a volcanic highland. Explain why the soil there helps their bananas grow well.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.

realProblems.prompt: People in a coastal town like Lamu practise moving to higher ground quickly. Why might this be important if there is a strong earthquake out at sea?
realProblems.answer: 1 Old lava and ash from the volcano break down into very rich, fertile soil. This gives the banana plants plenty of the nutrients they need to grow well.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 Old lava and ash from the volcano break down into very rich, fertile soil. This gives the banana plants plenty of the nutrients they need to grow well.

realProblems.prompt: Halima sees flamingos feeding at a Rift Valley lake. Explain how their beaks are suited to finding food in the shallow water.
realProblems.answer: 2 A strong earthquake under the sea can cause a tsunami. Moving quickly to higher ground before the giant wave arrives can keep people safe from the flooding.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 A strong earthquake under the sea can cause a tsunami. Moving quickly to higher ground before the giant wave arrives can keep people safe from the flooding.

realProblems.prompt: A camel and a coral-reef fish both live in Kenya, but in very different habitats. Describe each habitat and give one adaptation that suits each animal to its home.
realProblems.answer: 3 A flamingo has a long, flat beak that works like a sieve. It sweeps its beak through the shallow water and strains out tiny plants and animals to eat.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 A flamingo has a long, flat beak that works like a sieve. It sweeps its beak through the shallow water and strains out tiny plants and animals to eat.

realProblems.prompt: Volcanoes and earthquakes both happen along the Great Rift Valley. Using what you know about the Earth's crust, explain why these two things happen in the same places.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.

realProblems.prompt: A model of the Earth, such as a cut mango, is useful but not perfect. Describe two ways the mango is a good model and two ways it is a poor model of the real Earth.
realProblems.answer: 1 Both happen where the crust is cracked and the rocks are moving. Along the Rift Valley the crust is being pulled apart, which creates cracks. Magma can rise through the cracks to make volcanoes, and the moving, slipping rocks cause earthquakes - so the two go together in the same places.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 Both happen where the crust is cracked and the rocks are moving. Along the Rift Valley the crust is being pulled apart, which creates cracks. Magma can rise through the cracks to make volcanoes, and the moving, slipping rocks cause earthquakes - so the two go together in the same places.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three main layers of the Earth in order from the outside to the centre.
Why can scientists not simply cut the Earth open to study its inside? What do they use instead?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 Crust, mantle, core. (From outside to centre.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between magma and lava?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 The Earth is far too big and deep to cut open, and the deepest hole reaches only about 12 km. So scientists use models instead.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words what causes an earthquake.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Magma is melted rock while it is still inside the Earth. Lava is the same melted rock after it has erupted onto the surface.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between the focus and the epicentre of an earthquake?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 Rocks in the crust push and rub against each other. Pressure builds until they suddenly slip, releasing energy that travels as waves and shakes the ground.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: How does an earthquake under the sea cause a tsunami?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 The focus is where the earthquake begins inside the crust. The epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.

questions.question: The thin, solid outer layer of the Earth where we live is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: crust The crust is the thin, solid outer rocky layer we live on.

questions.question: The very hot centre of the Earth is made mostly of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: iron and nickel The core is made of these two metals, not rock.

questions.question: Melted rock inside the Earth is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: magma Melted rock inside the Earth is magma.

questions.question: Once melted rock erupts onto the surface, we call it:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: lava The same melted rock is called lava once it erupts onto the surface.

questions.question: The bowl-shaped hole at the top of a volcano is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: crater The crater is the bowl-shaped hole at the top of the volcano.

questions.question: An earthquake is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a shaking of the ground 'Quake' means shake; an earthquake is the ground shaking.

questions.question: The point on the surface directly above where an earthquake begins is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: epicentre The epicentre is on the surface directly above the focus.

questions.question: A huge sea wave caused by an underwater earthquake is a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: tsunami A tsunami is a giant sea wave from an underwater earthquake or eruption.

questions.question: The natural home where an animal finds food and shelter is its:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: habitat A habitat is where an animal finds food, water and shelter.

questions.question: A flamingo's flat, sieve-like beak is best for:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straining tiny food from water The flamingo's flat beak works like a sieve.

questions.question: The giant crack in the Earth's crust running through East Africa is the:

questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: they make rich farming soil Broken-down lava and ash make very fertile soil.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can describe the three main layers of the Earth - the crust, the mantle and the core - and say what each layer is made of.
I can explain how scientists use models to understand the inside of the Earth, and why a model is never a perfect copy of the real thing.
I can describe how a volcano forms and name its main parts: the vent, the crater, the lava and the ash.
I can explain what causes an earthquake and how the shaking travels as waves to the surface.
I can describe how earthquakes under the sea can cause a tsunami (a giant sea wave).
I can explain how animals and plants are suited to their habitat, using local examples such as the savanna, the coral reef and the mangrove.
I can connect the Earth's hot inside layers to the volcanoes and earthquakes we see at the surface.
